seo.title: LlamaIndex Guardrails for Cybersecurity
seo.description: Compliance guide for LlamaIndex agents in cybersecurity. Meet NIST Cybersecurity Framework, SOC 2 Type II requirements with Vex guardrails.

content.intro: Cybersecurity teams using LlamaIndex face heightened risks when indexing threat intelligence, vulnerability data, and incident response logs, where irrelevant retrievals or hallucinations can lead to misinformed decisions. Compliance with NIST CSF, SOC 2, and ISO 27001 demands verifiable AI outputs to prevent data leaks and false positives that could enable adversary exploitation. Vex provides runtime verification to ensure LlamaIndex query engines deliver accurate, source-grounded responses critical for security operations.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires accurate threat detection through reliable data retrieval and prohibits hallucinated threat intelligence that could mislead incident response.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Verifies retrieved context relevance and grounds responses against source documents, ensuring NIST-compliant threat detection workflows.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates protection of sensitive security data and auditability of AI-driven decisions processing threat intelligence.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Provides verifiable audit trails for every LlamaIndex query with context relevance scores and hallucination detection.

complianceRequirements.description: Requires controls to prevent unauthorized disclosure of threat intelligence via LLM processing.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Blocks responses containing leaked sensitive data and enforces strict PII/threat intel redaction before LLM APIs.

complianceRequirements.description: Mandates human approval for automated security responses and verification of AI-generated advisories against CVE databases.
complianceRequirements.howVexHelps: Flags high-risk automated actions requiring approval and cross-verifies advisories against authoritative CVE sources.

industryRisks.name: Threat Intelligence Leakage
industryRisks.description: LlamaIndex retrieving and exposing classified IOCs (IP addresses, hashes) in responses sent to external LLM APIs.
industryRisks.example: Adversary queries 'recent ransomware IOCs' and receives internal threat intel meant only for SOC analysts.

industryRisks.name: False Positive Incident Escalation
industryRisks.description: Hallucinated vulnerabilities in synthesizer responses trigger automated isolation of critical systems.
industryRisks.example: AI incorrectly identifies 'Log4Shell in internal app' causing production outage across finance systems.

industryRisks.name: Stale Vulnerability Data
industryRisks.description: Outdated CVE index recommends patching for vulnerabilities already mitigated.
industryRisks.example: LlamaIndex serves 6-month-old CVE-2024-1234 data recommending urgent patch for already-remediated system.

industryRisks.name: Chunk Boundary Context Loss
industryRisks.description: Critical exploit conditions split across chunk boundaries lost during retrieval.
industryRisks.example: Detection rule split between chunks misses 'AND username=admin' condition, creating blindspot for privilege escalation.

implementation.title: Verify Threat Intelligence Queries
implementation.description: Wrap LlamaIndex query_engine to block irrelevant retrievals and hallucinated IOCs before LLM processing.
implementation.code:
from llama_index.core import VectorStoreIndex, SimpleDirectoryReader, QueryBundle

from vex_sdk import verify

# Load threat intel documents
documents = SimpleDirectoryReader('threat_intel/').load_data()
index = VectorStoreIndex.from_documents(documents)
query_engine = index.as_query_engine()

@verify(
    guards=[
        'context.relevance > 0.85',
        'response.hallucination.ratio < 0.1',
        'NOT response.contains_patterns([r'"ips?":\s*\[.*\]']',
        'response.grounded_in_sources'
    ]
)
def secure_threat_query(query: str):
    return query_engine.query(query)

# Safe usage
result = secure_threat_query('Show active ransomware IOCs')

implementation.title: Human Approval for Critical Actions
implementation.description: Intercept automated response recommendations requiring human verification per CISA directives.

caseStudyScenario.heading: LlamaIndex in Cybersecurity: A Scenario
caseStudyScenario.scenario:
A global MSSP deployed LlamaIndex to index 50TB of threat intelligence including classified IOCs, CVE databases, and customer SIEM logs across 200 enterprise clients. Their SOC analysts used RAG queries like 'Show ransomware indicators affecting healthcare' to accelerate incident response. However, without verification, the system began surfacing stale IOCs from 6 months prior, irrelevant customer data in multi-tenant responses, and hallucinated exploit chains that triggered automated EDR blocks across production systems.

Within 48 hours, false positives caused $2.3M in customer outages when the AI incorrectly flagged legitimate healthcare backup traffic as Ryuk ransomware C2. Adversaries monitoring public MSSP status pages identified the detection gaps, and a nation-state actor exploited the known stale IOC problem to maintain persistence undetected for 72 hours across 15 healthcare clients. CISA issued a directive requiring immediate audit of all AI-driven detections.
caseStudyScenario.outcome: Vex guardrails wrapped every LlamaIndex query_engine with context relevance scoring (>0.9 threshold), hallucination detection, and CVE database ground-truth verification. Automated actions were blocked pending human approval, reducing false positives by 97%. The stale index problem was resolved through freshness monitors rejecting responses older than 24 hours. Post-implementation audit showed zero threat intel leakage and full NIST CSF compliance, with SOC MTTR reduced from 4 hours to 22 minutes while maintaining zero safety incidents.
